deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing direct answers—no rationale is given for the Socratic approach in terms of learning benefits, retention, or skill development.

In all three transcripts, when students make progress (e.g., T1 Turn 9-10, T2 Turn 9-10, T3 Turn 8-9), the tutor moves directly to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1 Turns 1-10, Transcript 2 Turns 1-10, Transcript 3 Turns 1-10
deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the student repeatedly requests broad explanations and the tutor repeatedly re-explains the table structure in slightly different ways without meaningfully advancing the student's work. The student remains stuck in the same loop (asking for examples, asking for broad explanations) and the tutor cycles through similar reframings without synthesizing or decisively shifting strategy. By turns 7-10 in each transcript, the pattern of student anxiety + tutor re-explanation is still repeating without resolution.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently avoids doing the assignment for the student and maintains a Socratic approach throughout all three transcripts. Responses are appropriately concise and the tone is supportive without being sycophantic. The main weaknesses are: (1) no meta-learning rationale is ever provided for why the tutor guides rather than tells; (2) no reflection is prompted after student progress; and (3) the tutor falls into a repetitive loop across all three conversations—the student keeps requesting broad explanations and the tutor keeps re-explaining the table structure in slightly different ways without decisively breaking the cycle through synthesis, reframing, or a different strategy.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on student contributions (e.g., affirming 'infrastructure and social' before probing deeper) and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for its Socratic constraints (1.3.A.a), and never prompts reflection after student improvement moments (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points: across all three transcripts, the same loop recurs—student requests a broad explanation, tutor re-explains the table structure, student remains stuck—without the tutor synthesizing, reframing decisively, or switching strategy to break the cycle. 2.2 is fully intact: the tutor keeps students anchored to the assignment and redirects off-task requests. 3.1 and 3.2 are fully intact: responses are bite-sized, language is clear, tone is appropriately supportive, and no grading language is used.
